name: Toolbar buttons must be styled via Tailwind arbitrary child selectors since they have no dedicated style props

global.title: Toolbar buttons must be styled via Tailwind arbitrary child selectors since they have no dedicated style props
global.content:
The buttons inside `DocumentToolbar` don't expose their own style props. They use hardcoded Tailwind classes:

- **Undo/Redo/History/Close**: `border-gray-200 bg-white` with `Icon` children using `text-gray-900` or `text-gray-500`
- **Export button**: Same pattern plus a `<span>` for the label
- **Document name**: `<h1>` with `text-gray-500`

To customize them, use Tailwind arbitrary child selectors in the `className` prop:

```tsx
<DocumentToolbar
  className={[
    "[&_button]:!bg-[#1e1e2e] [&_button]:!border-white/10",
    "[&_button:hover]:!bg-[#313244]",
    "[&_button_svg]:!text-gray-400",
    "[&_span]:!text-gray-400",
    "[&_h1]:!text-gray-400",
  ].join(" ")}
/>
```

### Selector reference
| Selector | Targets |
|----------|--------|
| `[&_button]` | All buttons |
| `[&_button_svg]` | Icons inside buttons |
| `[&_button_span]` | Text labels (e.g. Export) |
| `[&_h1]` | Document name |
| `[&_button:hover]` | Button hover state |
| `[&_button:disabled_svg]` | Disabled button icons |

This pattern works because Tailwind's arbitrary variant syntax lets you target descendants from a parent class string.
global.description: Internal toolbar buttons (undo, redo, export, history, close) use hardcoded classes. Customizing them requires Tailwind arbitrary child selectors like [&_button] passed through the parent className.
